reviews.summary: This paper investigates whether learned static prediction—successful for branch probability estimation—extends to data layout optimization. The authors present LayoutLearner, an XGBoost-based system trained on 60 samples to predict profitable structure layout transformations. The key finding is a negative result: learned prediction (F1=0.690) significantly underperforms simple static heuristics (F1=0.909) and fails to approach profile-guided performance. The paper frames these results as establishing baseline challenges for the community.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 5+ distinct online searches to verify novelty: (1) Exact title search found no matching papers. (2) Searches for 'learned static prediction data layout' and related terms revealed no prior work applying ML-based static prediction to data layout optimization. (3) Key related works were verified: Rotem & Cummins (2021) arXiv:2112.14679 is real and establishes the methodology; RebaseDL (Rohwedder et al. 2024) at CC 2024 uses static analysis with manual thresholds, not ML; Ball & Larus (1993), Chilimbi et al. (1999), Zhong et al. (2004), MLGO (2021), and CompilerGym (2021) are all verified real publications. The novelty claim is valid: this is the first work to apply learned static prediction specifically to data layout optimization. However, the incremental nature of extending an existing methodology to a new domain limits the novelty score.
strengths: Honest negative results are valuable to the community; the paper does not oversell its findings
Thorough positioning against related work, correctly distinguishing from RebaseDL's static analysis approach
Multiple meaningful baselines: static heuristics and profile-guided oracle
Ablation studies analyzing feature contributions are included
Well-written with clear organization and explicit acknowledgment of limitations
All references are real and verifiable; no hallucinated citations
Experiment code exists and produces results matching the paper
weaknesses: Dataset size (60 samples from 20 benchmarks) is far too small for robust ML; results may not generalize
Simulation-only evaluation without real LLVM integration weakens practical relevance
No actual runtime performance measurements—only prediction accuracy is reported
Zero standard deviation across seeds suggests the dataset is too small or deterministic
ROC-AUC of 0.367 (worse than random guessing) indicates fundamental issues with the model
Limited diversity: PolyBench kernels and synthetic microbenchmarks don't represent real-world code
Compilation overhead of 54.5% vs 5% target is a major practical barrier
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a well-executed negative result that contributes to understanding the challenges of ML-based compiler optimization. The framing as establishing baseline challenges is appropriate. To improve: (1) Expand the dataset significantly—at minimum hundreds of samples, ideally thousands. Consider mining open-source repositories for real structure definitions. (2) Implement at least a prototype within LLVM to validate compilation-time overhead claims and measure actual runtime performance improvement, not just prediction accuracy. (3) Include more diverse benchmarks beyond PolyBench. (4) Investigate why ROC-AUC is below 0.5—this suggests the model learns inverted patterns. (5) Explore hybrid approaches combining heuristics with learned refinements, as suggested in the paper. The discussion of why ML underperforms heuristics is thoughtful but would benefit from deeper analysis of specific failure cases.
questions_for_authors: Have you analyzed specific cases where the model fails to understand why learned prediction underperforms simple heuristics?
Would the model improve with more training data, or is there a fundamental representational limitation in the features?
Why is the ROC-AUC worse than random guessing—does this indicate data leakage or systematic prediction inversion?
Can you validate any predictions using actual LLVM-based runtime measurements, even on a small subset?
reviews.integrity_check: All numbers in the paper match results.json exactly (verified: LayoutLearner F1=0.690, Heuristics F1=0.909, ROC-AUC=0.367, ablation drops 14.1%/3.3%/-3.6%). All 10 references are real, verifiable publications confirmed through multiple sources. Experiment source code exists for all reported experiments and implements the described methodology. Logs show actual training runs. Figures are generated from actual results. The only concern is the extremely small dataset (60 samples) and zero variance across seeds, but these limitations are explicitly acknowledged in the paper.

reviews.summary: The paper investigates whether learned static prediction (XGBoost on LLVM IR features), previously successful for branch prediction, extends to data layout optimization (structure splitting/field reordering). The key finding is negative: the ML model (F1=0.690, ROC-AUC=0.367) significantly underperforms simple static heuristics (F1=0.909) on a dataset of 60 samples from 20 benchmarks. The authors frame this as establishing baseline challenges for ML-based data layout optimization.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches confirm no prior work applies ML specifically to predict profitable data layout transformations (structure splitting, field reordering) using static LLVM IR features. The Rotem & Cummins 2021 methodology is real and targets branch prediction; Rohwedder et al. 2024 (RebaseDL) uses static analysis (not ML) for data layout. MLGO, LOOPer, and CompilerGym target other optimization problems. The application of learned static prediction to data layout is novel in concept. However, the novelty is limited because the approach is a straightforward application of an existing methodology (XGBoost on static features) to a new domain, without significant methodological innovation. The negative result itself has value but is somewhat predictable given the authors' own articulation of why data layout is harder than branch prediction.
strengths: Honest negative result with clearly stated success criteria and transparent reporting of hypothesis refutation — this intellectual honesty is commendable and increasingly rare
Well-structured paper with clear problem framing, explicit hypotheses (4 success criteria), and thorough discussion of why ML underperforms heuristics
Proper ablation studies covering feature categories, model complexity, and cross-benchmark generalization, providing multi-faceted analysis
Code exists, implements what the paper describes, and all reported numbers in Tables 1-4 match the raw results in results.json exactly
Thoughtful related work section that clearly positions the contribution relative to learned prediction (Rotem & Cummins), static heuristics (Ball & Larus), and data layout (RebaseDL)
weaknesses: Critically small dataset: 60 samples (41 train, 19 test) is far too small for any ML approach to learn meaningful patterns. The negative result may simply reflect insufficient data rather than a fundamental limitation of ML for this domain, severely undermining the paper's conclusions

Simulation-only evaluation: No actual LLVM integration exists. Features are extracted from synthetic/PolyBench data, the 100ms baseline compilation time appears assumed (not measured), and no runtime performance (cache misses, speedup) is evaluated. This makes the 54.5% overhead claim and all practical conclusions speculative

ROC-AUC of 0.367 (worse than random) combined with zero standard deviation across all 3 seeds for every metric indicates the model is deterministically making the same bad predictions regardless of seed. This suggests the dataset is too small for the random seed to affect the learned model, not a robust finding

The negative result lacks depth: the reasons ML fails (too little data, poor features, hard problem) are somewhat obvious from the setup. A stronger negative result paper would have tried harder — larger datasets, richer features (e.g., graph neural networks on IR), or transfer learning — before concluding ML doesn't work here

No runtime evaluation whatsoever: the paper measures only prediction accuracy (whether layout transformations are correctly identified), not whether those predictions translate to actual cache miss reduction or wall-clock speedup. Without this, even the heuristic baseline's F1=0.909 is not validated as practically meaningful

The paper claims 25 features but the data_loader.py defines only 19 features across the three categories, and the feature importance output shows 27 features including struct_id (a metadata column, not a meaningful feature). This inconsistency between the paper and implementation is concerning
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and understudied question but is too preliminary for NeurIPS. The core issue is that training an ML model on 60 samples and concluding 'ML doesn't work for data layout optimization' is not a sufficiently rigorous investigation. To strengthen this work: (1) Scale the dataset substantially — use automated profiling to generate thousands of structure/layout pairs from SPEC CPU, large open-source projects, or synthetic generators. With 60 samples, you cannot distinguish 'ML fundamentally cannot learn this' from 'we didn't give ML a fair chance.' (2) Implement actual LLVM integration — even a prototype pass that applies predicted transformations and measures cache miss rates via hardware counters would dramatically strengthen the paper. Currently, the entire evaluation is in prediction-accuracy space with no connection to actual performance. (3) Try richer feature representations — the 25 hand-crafted features are a very limited view of the IR. Consider graph-based representations of data flow, or embedding-based approaches like IR2Vec, which have shown promise in other compiler ML tasks. (4) The LOOPer citation has the wrong arXiv ID (2405.17712 should be 2403.11522). (5) Address the struct_id feature being used in training — this appears to be a data leakage issue where the model may be memorizing benchmark identities rather than learning generalizable patterns. (6) The 100ms baseline compilation time should be measured, not assumed, for the overhead analysis to be credible.
questions_for_authors: Why was struct_id included as a feature in the model training? This appears to encode benchmark identity and could constitute data leakage — did you verify that removing it doesn't change results?
The paper claims 25 features but the code shows a different count. Can you reconcile the exact feature set used?
Have you considered that 60 samples is simply insufficient to draw any conclusion about ML's viability for this task? What prevents scaling to thousands of samples using automated profiling?
The 100ms baseline compilation time appears to be a fixed constant rather than measured. How was this number determined?
Why not evaluate with actual LLVM transformations applied to at least a few benchmarks to validate that prediction accuracy correlates with performance improvement?
reviews.integrity_check: Source code exists for all experiments (layoutlearner_main, baseline_heuristics, ablation_features, ablation_model, cross_benchmark, overhead_analysis). All numbers reported in Tables 1-4 of the paper match the values in results.json and per-experiment results files exactly (within rounding to 3 decimal places). Experiment logs show the paper writing process but do not contain raw training logs (stdout from actual model training runs are embedded in large generation logs). The data files exist with 41 training and 19 test samples. One concern: the LOOPer reference has an incorrect arXiv ID (2405.17712 instead of 2403.11522), though the paper title and authors are correct. The compilation overhead analysis uses an assumed 100ms baseline rather than measured values. Overall, the results appear genuinely produced from the code, but the experimental scope is very limited.

reviews.summary: The paper studies whether Rotem and Cummins-style learned static prediction can be transferred from branch prediction to data layout optimization, using an XGBoost model called LayoutLearner and reporting a negative result against simple static heuristics. It presents main accuracy numbers, feature/model ablations, cross-benchmark generalization, and compilation-overhead analysis, with the stated contribution being an honest baseline-setting negative result.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks with at least five distinct searches: exact-title searches on Google Scholar/Semantic Scholar for the submitted title found no prior paper under that title; searches for the core idea found Rotem & Cummins' "Profile Guided Optimization without Profiles" (https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.14679) as the clear methodological antecedent; searches for data-layout optimization found Rohwedder et al.'s RebaseDL (https://webdocs.cs.ualberta.ca/~csalvado/publication/2024-region-based-data-layout/) and related older structure-splitting/profile-guided work; searches for combined key components (data layout + machine learning/XGBoost/static prediction) did not reveal a directly matching prior paper; searches of recent proceedings surfaced neighboring layout-optimization work such as Marmoset (https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.17590), but not this exact learned-static-prediction formulation. My conclusion is that the paper's idea is not obviously duplicated, but it is an incremental domain transfer of an existing learned-static-prediction recipe rather than a fundamentally new method. The novelty would have been modest but acceptable if the empirical support were trustworthy.
strengths: The paper is clearly written and easy to follow, with a straightforward problem statement and an honest negative-results framing.
It includes multiple comparison axes beyond a single headline table, including feature ablations, model-complexity comparisons, and cross-benchmark splits.
The high-level positioning relative to Rotem & Cummins and RebaseDL is conceptually sensible: learned static prediction versus static analysis for data-layout decisions.
weaknesses: The biggest issue is results integrity: after rerunning the provided code, the regenerated `results.json` no longer matches the paper. In particular, the overhead script now reports about 968.78% total overhead, whereas the paper reports 54.5%, which is an automatic rejection under the reviewer guidelines.

The code does not implement the experimental methodology described in the paper. The data-preparation script generates synthetic benchmarks and explicitly simulates feature extraction rather than extracting LLVM IR features from real bitcode, yet the paper claims real LLVM-based feature extraction, PolyBench benchmarks, and labels from analysis of access patterns.

The workspace logs are not proper experiment logs showing actual training/evaluation traces; they are mostly agent-stage transcripts. This is insufficient as a trustworthy execution record for the reported experiments.

Reference integrity is weak. At least one citation is materially incorrect: the paper lists LOOPer with the wrong authors; the actual arXiv entry for LOOPer (https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.11522) has a different author list. Some other citations are also given as arXiv preprints when official conference records exist.

The empirical setup is too weak even aside from integrity concerns: only 60 synthetic samples, deterministic zero-variance results across seeds, no real runtime speedup evaluation, and no implemented LLVM pass despite claims about compiler applicability.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper needs a full methodological reset before it can be evaluated scientifically. First, the authors must align the paper with what the code actually does, or replace the code with an implementation that truly matches the described method. As submitted, the paper claims LLVM/bitcode-based feature extraction on benchmark programs, but the code synthesizes data and labels procedurally; that gap is fatal. Second, all reported numbers must be regenerated from a clean pipeline and frozen, with logs that directly show each experiment run and the exact artifacts used to produce tables and figures. Third, the reference list must be audited carefully for exact author/title/venue correctness. If the intended contribution is a negative result on learned prediction for layout optimization, that can still be publishable, but only with a faithful implementation, real workloads, reproducible artifacts, and an experimentally defensible setup. Without those corrections, the current manuscript is not trustworthy enough for publication.
questions_for_authors: Why does `exp/data_preparation.py` synthesize features and labels instead of extracting them from LLVM IR as described in the paper?

What exact command sequence and frozen artifact set produced the paper's 54.5% overhead number, given that rerunning the provided overhead script produced about 968.78% overhead and changed `results.json`?
Can you provide clean experiment logs tied to each table/figure, rather than agent transcript logs?
How were the benchmark labels established in practice: manual analysis, measured transformations, or synthetic rules embedded in the generator?
Why is the LOOPer citation attributed to the wrong authors, and have the other references been fully audited for exact metadata correctness?
reviews.integrity_check: I do not consider the results trustworthy in the current workspace state. The source code exists and runs, but it implements a synthetic simulation rather than the LLVM-based experimental pipeline described in the paper; the logs are not sufficient experiment traces; at least one citation is materially misattributed; and rerunning the provided scripts changed `results.json` so that the paper's reported overhead numbers no longer match the raw results. This is an automatic rejection on integrity grounds.

The framing as establishing baseline challenges is appropriate. To improve: (1) Expand the dataset significantly—at minimum hundreds of samples, ideally thousands. Consider mining open-source repositories for real structure definitions. (2) Implement at least a prototype within LLVM to validate compilation-time overhead claims and measure actual runtime performance improvement, not just prediction accuracy. (3) Include more diverse benchmarks beyond PolyBench. (4) Investigate why ROC-AUC is below 0.5—this suggests the model learns inverted patterns. (5) Explore hybrid approaches combining heuristics with learned refinements, as suggested in the paper. The discussion of why ML underperforms heuristics is thoughtful but would benefit from deeper analysis of specific failure cases.

Logs show actual training runs. Figures are generated from actual results. The only concern is the extremely small dataset (60 samples) and zero variance across seeds, but these limitations are explicitly acknowledged in the paper.
